topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the page turning was the first thing that snagged my attention. It was a soft, almost inaudible rustle coming from the corner of Liam’s desk, the one I’d been meticulously guarding. I froze mid-sip of my lukewarm coffee, my hand hovering near the mug as if it were a live wire. Slowly, dread pooled in my stomach, tightening into a cold, hard knot. I crept over, my bare feet making no sound on the worn carpet. There, spread open across Liam’s textbook, was my journal. His finger, a long, pale extension, was tracing a line of my handwriting, pausing, then moving on. A sharp, breathless sound escaped my throat, a small, strangled noise. I snatched the book back, my fingers trembling so violently I almost dropped it. When I finally turned, Liam was staring blankly at the ceiling, a faint, unsettling smile playing on his lips. The air in the room felt suddenly thin, suffocating.

[story 2]

It started with a misplaced bookmark, a tiny sliver of paper tucked into the middle of my private diary. I found it later, tucked beneath a stack of philosophy notes, and a wave of nausea washed over me. When I opened it, the entries weren't mine. They were Liam's handwriting, his thoughtful, meandering thoughts about existential dread and late-night study sessions. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat echoing in the quiet dorm room. I couldn't look away. I reread a passage, the ink seeming to burn through my vision. I backed away slowly, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. I needed air, desperately, but my legs felt rooted to the floor. I watched Liam across the room, his posture relaxed, completely unaware of the seismic shift that had just occurred between us. The silence between us was deafening, filled only with the roaring chaos inside my own head.

[story 3]

I walked into the room expecting the usual organized clutter, but the sight that greeted me made my vision swim. Liam was sitting at his desk, bathed in the pale glow of his laptop screen, and his gaze was fixed on my journal. He wasn't just looking; he was reading, his expression unreadable, yet somehow intensely focused. A sudden, overwhelming surge of panic seized my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. My hands clenched into fists so tight my nails dug crescent moons into my palms. I took a stumbling step forward, my voice coming out as a strained whisper, "Liam, what are you doing?" The sound was thin, reedy, utterly unlike my usual steady tone. He looked up, and for a split second, I saw something flicker in his eyes—a flicker of something I couldn't decipher—before he quickly looked back down, a sudden, sharp intake of breath escaping him that felt like a physical blow. I couldn't move; I just stood there, paralyzed by the sheer wrongness of the scene.
